topic: HL Regional Option: History of Europe
subject: history

items.q: Why was Jenner's smallpox vaccination significant in 1796?
items.correct: He showed cowpox material could prevent smallpox safely — the first effective vaccine, though he did not know why it worked; germ theory came nearly a century later.
items.misconception: Jenner discovered germs and used them to make vaccines.
items.random_wrong: Jenner used penicillin to cure smallpox.
items.why: Jenner worked empirically, not from germ theory which was Pasteur's. Students collapse the discoveries together; vaccination predates the science explaining it.

items.q: What did the 1832 Great Reform Act actually do?
items.correct: It abolished rotten boroughs, redistributed seats to industrial towns, and extended the vote to about 1 in 5 adult men — still far short of democracy.
items.misconception: The 1832 Reform Act gave the vote to all working men.
items.random_wrong: The 1832 Reform Act allowed women to vote for the first time.
items.why: Working men got the vote only in 1867 (urban) and 1884 (rural). Students compress all 19th-century franchise extensions into one act.

items.q: Why did Luther's 95 Theses spread so quickly in 1517?
items.correct: The printing press allowed rapid reproduction; existing critique of Church corruption and indulgence sales found ready audiences; German princes had political reasons to back Luther against Rome.
items.misconception: Luther's ideas spread because he was the first to criticise the Catholic Church.
items.random_wrong: Luther's ideas spread because the Pope endorsed them.
items.why: Critics like Hus and Wycliffe predated Luther by over a century. The printing press and political conditions are what made Luther succeed where earlier reformers failed.

items.q: Which Bolshevik slogan summarised Lenin's April Theses?
items.why: It's Peace, Land, Bread. “Workers of the world unite” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What was the immediate cause of the February Revolution?
items.why: It's Bread shortages in Petrograd. “German invasion” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did the Russian Civil War end?
items.why: Exact answer: 1921 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
